engineCodes.h2: Skoda Engine Codes & Average Rebuilt Prices (UK Supply)
engineCodes.h3: Compare costs for every major Skoda engine — EA211, EA288 and EA888 series. All prices are typical UK market ranges for professionally rebuilt engines with warranty. Click "Get Quote" on any engine code to receive tailored prices from our network of UK Skoda specialists.

groups.name: EA211 Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: EA211 1.0 TSI and 1.2 TSI engines are known for early timing chain stretch and tensioner failure — particularly on 2012–2016 units. Our rebuilt EA211 engines include revised timing chain kits and upgraded tensioners. Carbon buildup on intake valves is also common due to direct injection; rebuilt units are fully decarbonised before assembly.

groups.name: EA288 Diesel Family
groups.failureNote: EA288 1.6 and 2.0 TDI engines suffer from DPF regeneration failures (short trips), EGR cooler leaks, and clogged intake manifolds. Our rebuilt EA288 engines include professionally cleaned DPF/EGR systems or replacement units, tested injectors, and new timing belts.

groups.name: EA888 Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: EA888 Gen1 and Gen2 engines (1.8/2.0 TFSI) are notorious for timing chain tensioner failure and oil pump drive degradation. Gen3 units (CRTD, CHHA) improved reliability but still suffer from carbon buildup and water pump leaks. Our rebuilt EA888 units include revised chain tensioners, upgraded oil pump drives (where applicable), and fully cleaned intake valves.

groups.name: Legacy MPI & Older TDI
groups.failureNote: 1.9 TDI engines (PD unit injector) are robust but suffer from injector loom failure, camshaft wear (on BRM/BLS variants), and turbo actuator issues. MPI petrol engines are generally reliable but age-related wear (head gaskets, piston rings) drives replacements on high-mileage units

commonProblems.h2: Common Skoda Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Skoda’s popularity across UK fleets, family hatchbacks, and tradespeople means high mileages are common — and so is wear on its VW Group engines. The EA211 1.2/1.4 TSI and EA288 1.6/2.0 TDI families dominate replacement requests on EngineMarket, primarily for timing chain wear and diesel emission system failures. Every rebuilt Skoda engine supplied through our network includes upgraded components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below we break down the four most costly Skoda engine failures, what genuine repair costs look like at a dealer versus a specialist, and when replacement is the only sensible choice.

problems.h4: EA211 / 1.2 TSI & 1.4 TSI Timing Chain Failure – The High‑Mileage Achilles’ Heel
problems.affectedModels: Skoda Fabia (2010–2014, EA211 1.2 TSI – CBZA/CBZB); Skoda Rapid, Octavia, Yeti (2012–2015, 1.2/1.4 TSI – CJZD, CZCA, CHYB, CZEA)
problems.rootCause: Early EA211 engines use a single-row timing chain with a poorly tensioned hydraulic tensioner. As oil degrades or service intervals extend, the tensioner bleeds pressure, allowing chain slap, guide breakage, and eventual jumping of timing.
problems.recommendation: If you catch the chain rattle early (cold start rattle that lasts 1–2 seconds) and compression is still good, the minor kit replacement from a specialist is the far better value — typically saving £400–£700 over a dealer. However, if the engine has already jumped time, do not waste money on a chain kit alone. On a Fabia or Rapid worth £2,000–£4,000, a rebuilt EA211 with warranty is the safer, more economic path.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 1.2 TSI / 1.4 TSI (EA211) engine prices for your Fabia, Rapid, or Yeti

problems.h4: EA288 1.6 & 2.0 TDI DPF / EGR Blockage – The MOT and AdBlue Nightmare
problems.affectedModels: Skoda Octavia (2013–2020, 1.6 TDI – CLCA, DBKA, CRVA; 2.0 TDI – CKFC, CRLB, CRMB, DFGA); Skoda Superb, Karoq, Kodiaq (2015–2021, 1.6/2.0 TDI)
problems.rootCause: Frequent short trips prevent passive DPF regeneration. EGR valve soots up, followed by DPF soot loading, differential pressure sensor failures, and eventually backpressure that stresses turbo and rings. AdBlue systems add injector crystallisation on later EA288 evo engines.
problems.recommendation: For a short‑hop car, a forced regen is often a temporary fix that returns within weeks. We recommend either the intermediate tier if you’re selling the car soon, or the full rebuilt engine if you plan to keep it. A specialist rebuilt EA288 with a warranty removes uncertainty around hidden turbo damage — a risk main dealers rarely address during DPF swaps.
problems.cta: → Compare DPF‑ready rebuilt 1.6 / 2.0 TDI engines for Octavia, Karoq, and Superb

problems.h4: 1.4 TSI / 1.8 TFSI (EA888 Gen 1 & 2) Carbon Buildup on Intake Valves – The Direct‑Injection Stumble
problems.affectedModels: Skoda Octavia vRS (2009–2013, 2.0 TFSI – CCZA); Skoda Superb (2008–2015, 1.8 TSI – CDAA, CJSA); Skoda Yeti (2009–2015, 1.8 TSI – CZPA)
problems.rootCause: Direct injection sprays fuel directly into the cylinder, so no fuel washes over intake valves. This allows crankcase oil vapour deposits to bake onto valves, restricting airflow, causing cold misfires, rough idle, and eventually loss of power.
problems.recommendation: Carbon cleaning is not a repair — it’s maintenance. On a high‑mileage Octavia vRS or Superb 1.8 TSI, the walnut blast from a specialist is the clear winner, costing less than half of dealer labour. Do not replace the engine simply due to carbon. Only choose the full replacement if you have confirmed compression loss, damaged valves, or the engine has also suffered timing chain failure.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 1.8 TSI / 2.0 TFSI engine prices with upgraded PCV and warranty

problems.h4: 2.0 TDI (EA288 & Older) Injector / High‑Pressure Pump Failure – The Common‑Rail Contamination Risk
problems.affectedModels: Skoda Octavia (2005–2012, 2.0 TDI – BKD, BMN); Skoda Superb (2008–2015, 2.0 TDI – CFFB, CLJA); Skoda Yeti (2009–2017, 2.0 TDI – CFHC, CLCB)
problems.rootCause: Piezo injectors wear and leak internally, or the high‑pressure fuel pump (HPFP) sheds swarf into the fuel rail. Contaminated fuel returns to the tank and takes out all injectors, the pump, and sometimes the rail.
problems.recommendation: If you have a single injector code and no metal in the fuel filter, replace all four injectors (never one) at a specialist — dealer price is unjustifiably high. But if the high‑pressure pump has failed, do not attempt an intermediate repair. The contamination is already systemic. On a Superb or Octavia worth £3k–£5k, a fully rebuilt engine with a new HPFP and injectors is the only way to avoid a second catastrophic failure within months.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for common‑rail rebuilt 2.0 TDI engines with new HPFP and injectors

problems.h4: 1.2 TSI / 1.4 TSI High Oil Consumption – Piston Ring & Oil Control Failure
problems.affectedModels: Skoda Fabia, Rapid, Octavia (2010–2015, 1.2 TSI – CBZA, CBZB); Skoda Yeti (2010–2015, 1.4 TSI – CAXA, CAXC)
problems.rootCause: Early TSI engines use low‑tension oil control rings that become clogged with carbonised oil due to extended service intervals and low‑speed driving. Ring sticking leads to bore glazing, high oil consumption (1L/1,000km), and eventual piston scoring.
problems.recommendation: This is a classic “repair or replace” borderline decision. On a Fabia 1.2 TSI worth less than £2,500, we recommend the full rebuilt engine from a specialist — the cost difference over an intermediate piston‑only repair is often only £300–£500 but yields a full warranty and upgraded rings. Do not pay dealer prices for a piston ring repair at this vehicle value; it nearly always exceeds market value.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt 1.2 TSI / 1.4 TSI engines with upgraded oil control rings

engineTypes.h2: Skoda Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Skoda engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle's age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Skoda Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,400 – £3,800 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Skoda Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as uprated timing chain kits and reinforced oil pump drives – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Skoda Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available.

types.title: Skoda Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for vRS performance models or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: Skoda Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn't had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models like the Fabia or Roomster where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Skoda Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Skoda specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £400 – £800 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Skoda engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code (EA211, EA288, EA888), supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Skoda model today.

engineSizes.h2: Skoda Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Skoda engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Skoda 1.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.2L three-cylinder TDI was Skoda's entry-level diesel, offered briefly in the Fabia and Roomster between 2010 and 2014. Based on the EA189 family (code BNM), this unit prioritised economy over power but suffered from DPF clogging on short journeys. Replacement demand remains low, but failure typically forces a full engine swap as parts availability is limited
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 1.2L diesel engines

items.title: Skoda 1.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.4L TDI three-cylinder (EA188 / EA189 family) appeared across Fabia, Rapid, and Roomster models from 2005 to 2015, using engine codes BNV, BMS, and AMF. While economical and torquey for its size, the unit is known for injector seal leaks, turbo actuator failure, and DPF issues in later versions. Many high‑mileage examples now require replacement rather than repair due to parts scarcity and labour costs
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 1.4L diesel engines

items.title: Skoda 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6L TDI (EA189 and EA288 families) is Skoda's most common diesel, fitted to Octavia, Superb, Yeti, Karoq, Kodiaq, Fabia, and Rapid between 2009 and 2024. Engine codes include CAYC, CLCA, CRKB, DBKA, and CRVA. The EA189 units suffer from EGR cooler fires, injector wiring faults, and DPF clogging, while EA288 engines add AdBlue injector crystallisation. Replacement demand is extremely high, especially for Octavia fleet cars
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Skoda 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0L TDI (EA189, EA288, EA288 evo) powers higher‑spec Octavia, Superb, Kodiaq, Karoq, and Yeti models from 2004 to present. Engine codes include BKD, BMN, CFHC, CFFB, CKFC, CRLB, DFGA, and DNUA. Known vulnerabilities vary: BKD injector loom failure, BMN DPF cracking, CFHC turbo vane sticking, and EA288 evo oil pump belt failure. This displacement dominates commercial and family fleet replacement searches
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 2.0L diesel engines
commonFailurePoints: EA288 evo oil pump belt failure

items.title: Skoda 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.0L three‑cylinder TSI (EA211 family) arrived in 2015 and now powers Fabia, Rapid, Scala, Kamiq, and Octavia. Engine codes include CHZB, CHZJ, DKLA, DKLC, and DLLA. This award‑winning unit is generally robust, but common replacement triggers include wet belt failure (early versions), injector failure, and high‑mileage turbo wastegate rattle. Replacement demand is rising as these engines age into the 80k–120k mile bracket
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L HTP (8V/12V) and later 1.2 TSI (EA111/EA211) spanned two decades across Fabia, Roomster, Octavia, Yeti, and Rapid. Early EA111 TSI engines (CBZA, CBZB) are notorious for timing chain tensioner failure, while the HTP is reliable but underpowered. Replacement demand remains strong for 2010–2014 Fabia and Yeti models where chain failure has bent valves
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.2L petrol engine prices
commonFailurePoints: EA111 TSI – timing chain tensioner failure

items.title: Skoda 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.4L TSI (EA111 and EA211 families) appeared in Octavia, Yeti, Superb, Fabia vRS, and Rapid between 2005 and 2018. Codes include CAXA, CAXC, CZCA, CZDA, and CHYB. The EA111 1.4 TSI is infamous for timing chain stretch and piston ring carbon clogging causing high oil consumption. EA211 units are more reliable but still suffer intake valve carbon buildup on direct‑injection versions. Replacement is common on 2008–2013 Octavia and Yeti
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L TSI Evo (EA211 evo family) launched in 2017 as a replacement for the 1.4 TSI, now found in Octavia, Superb, Karoq, Kamiq, Scala, and Fabia. Engine codes include DADA, DACA, DPBA, and DZTA. While more efficient than its predecessor, known failure points include kangarooing (cold start hesitation, mostly fixed by software), injector failure, and high‑mileage turbo wastegate issues. Replacement demand is still moderate but growing as fleet cars exceed 80k miles
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The naturally aspirated 1.6L MPI (EA211 family) is a simple, robust unit fitted to Octavia and Superb primarily for LPG‑friendly fleet markets. Engine codes include CWVA and CWVB. This engine is low‑stress and avoids many TSI problems, but high‑mileage units do require ignition coil packs, camshaft position sensors, and occasionally valve guide wear. Replacement demand is low but steady
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8L TSI (EA888 Gen1 and Gen2) appeared in Octavia, Superb, and Yeti between 2008 and 2015. Engine codes include CDAA, CJSA, BZB, and CZPA. Known weaknesses include timing chain tensioner failure (pre‑2012), intake valve carbon buildup, and water pump failure. Replacement demand is moderate, mostly from 2008–2012 Octavia and Superb examples where chain failure has caused valve damage
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0L TSI/TFSI (EA888 Gen1 through Gen4) powers performance Skoda models including Octavia vRS, Superb 2.0 TSI, Kodiaq vRS, and high‑spec Karoq. Engine codes include CCZA, CHHA, CJXC, CZPA, DNUA, and DNUE. Common failures vary by generation: Gen1 timing chain tensioner failure, Gen2 intake carbon buildup and failed intake manifolds, Gen3 water pump/thermostat housing leaks, Gen4 (current) minor oil consumption. The vRS badge makes this a high‑value replacement category
items.cta: Compare Skoda 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda Electric Motors
items.description: Skoda's first all‑electric model, the Enyaq iV, launched in 2020 using the Volkswagen Group MEB platform. It features a rear‑mounted permanent magnet synchronous motor in various power outputs (146bhp to 295bhp across 60/80/80x/vRS variants). Motor codes include EBP (132kW) and EBN (150kW). Electric motor failure is rare, with most driveability issues tracing back to battery management or inverter faults. Replacement is specialist‑only and typically not cost‑effective outside warranty. Motor code(s): EBP (132kW), EBN (150kW), EEV (220kW vRS)
items.cta: Enquire about Skoda electric motor repair

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Skoda Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Skoda engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Skoda specialists.

entries.title: CHZB 1.0 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The CHZB three-cylinder 1.0 TSI powers the Fabia (NJ, 2015–2021), Kamiq (2020–present), Scala (2020–present), and Octavia (5E FL, 2017–2020). Timing chain stretch and carbon buildup on intake valves are the main replacement drivers, particularly on cars used for short journeys. Our rebuilt CHZB units include a revised timing chain kit, fully decarbonised cylinder head, and new injector seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CHZB Skoda engine replacement

entries.title: DTSB 1.0 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The DTSB replaced earlier 1.0 TSI variants in the Fabia (NJ, 2018–2021), Scala, and Kamiq. Very similar to CHZB but with updated ECU mapping and a slightly revised valvetrain. Same failure points apply: chain stretch (less common than CHZB but still reported) and carbon buildup. Rebuilt DTSB engines include a fresh timing set, walnut-blasted valves, and new PCV system.

entries.title: CBZA 1.2 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The CBZA 1.2 TSI was fitted to the Fabia (NJ, 2010–2014), Rapid (NH, 2012–2019), Octavia (5E, 2012–2017), and Yeti (5L, 2009–2015). Timing chain tensioner failure (pre‑2014) is the #1 catastrophic failure — chain jump bends valves. High oil consumption from stuck piston rings is the #2 replacement driver. Our rebuilt CBZA units include an upgraded chain tensioner kit, new piston rings, and a fully serviced oil pump.

entries.title: CHYB 1.2 TSI Petrol
entries.description: Successor to CBZA in later Fabia (NJ, 2014–2018) and Rapid models. A revised tensioner reduced sudden failure rates, but chain stretch still occurs by 60,000–90,000 miles. Oil consumption remains an issue. Rebuilt CHYB units include a new timing chain set, revised oil separator, and decarbonised intake valves.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CHYB 1.2 TSI engine

entries.title: CRMB 1.6 MPI Petrol
entries.description: The CRMB is a naturally aspirated 1.6 MPI (multi-port injection) — no turbo, no direct injection. Fitted to Fabia (NJ), Octavia (5E), and Rapid. Extremely reliable, but high-mileage units (>120,000 miles) suffer piston ring wear, oil consumption, and occasional head gasket failure. Rebuilt CRMB engines include new piston rings, valve stem seals, timing chain, and water pump.

entries.title: CLCA 1.6 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The CLCA 1.6 TDI powers a huge range: Octavia (5E, 2012–2020), Fabia (NJ, 2014–2021), Rapid (NH), Yeti (5L, 2014–2017), and Karoq (NU). DPF regeneration failure (short trips), EGR cooler leaks, and intake manifold flap failure are the top three replacement triggers. Our rebuilt CLCA units include a cleaned or new DPF, replacement EGR valve/cooler, and a new timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CLCA 1.6 TDI engine replacement

entries.title: DBKA 1.6 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The DBKA is a later 1.6 TDI variant (Euro 6) fitted to Octavia (5E FL, 2016–2020), Fabia (NJ, 2017–2021), Karoq (NU), and Kodiaq (NS). Same EA288 architecture as CLCA — DPF/EGR failures dominate. Additionally, the AdBlue system (SCR) can fail, though this usually doesn't require a full engine replacement. Rebuilt DBKA engines include DPF clean/replacement, new EGR valve, and injector testing.

entries.title: CFHA 2.0 TDI 170 Diesel
entries.description: The CFHA 2.0 TDI (170hp) appeared in the Octavia vRS diesel (5E, 2013–2017), Superb (3T/3V), and Yeti (5L). High-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure sending metal debris through the injection system is a known catastrophic failure. DPF and EGR issues also occur. Our rebuilt CFHA units include a new HPFP, fully flushed fuel rail and injectors, DPF clean, and new timing belt.

entries.title: CRVC 2.0 TDI 150 Diesel
entries.description: The most common 2.0 TDI in the range — CRVC (150hp) powers the Octavia (5E), Superb (3V), Kodiaq (NS), Karoq (NU), and Yeti (5L). DPF/EGR failures, turbocharger sticking vanes, and intake manifold flap failure drive most replacements. Rebuilt CRVC units include a professionally cleaned DPF, new EGR cooler, turbo service or replacement, and new timing belt.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRVC 2.0 TDI engine

entries.title: CUNA 2.0 TDI 150 Diesel
entries.description: The CUNA is a later EA288 Evo variant for the Superb (3V, 2018–present) and Kodiaq (NS, 2018–present). Dual AdBlue injection and a more complex EGR system. Failures are less common, but DPF clogging (short trips) and EGR cooler leaks still occur. Rebuilt CUNA engines include DPF clean, new EGR system components, and updated software calibration where applicable.
entries.cta: Compare CUNA Skoda diesel engine prices

entries.title: CWZA 2.0 TDI 190 Diesel
entries.description: The top-output 2.0 TDI (190hp, twin‑dosed AdBlue) fitted to the Kodiaq (NS, 2017–present) and Superb (3V, 2018–present). Sticking turbo vanes (overboost/underboost codes) and DPF regeneration issues are the main replacement drivers. Rebuilt CWZA units include a reconditioned or new turbo, DPF clean, new EGR cooler, and revised oil separator.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CWZA 2.0 TDI 190 engine

entries.title: CCZA 1.8 TFSI Petrol
entries.description: The CCZA 1.8 TFSI (EA888 Gen2) was fitted to the Octavia (1Z, 2008–2013), Superb (3T, 2008–2015), and Yeti (5L, 2009–2015). Timing chain tensioner failure (pre‑2012) is catastrophic — chain jump bends valves. Carbon buildup on intake valves causes misfires. Our rebuilt CCZA units include the revised Gen3 timing chain tensioner, a full decarbonisation (walnut blasting), and a new water pump.

entries.title: CRGC 1.8 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The CRGC (EA888 Gen3, 180hp) appeared in the Octavia (5E, 2013–2017) and Superb (3V, 2015–2018). Improved timing chain tensioner but still suffers carbon buildup and water pump leaks. Oil pump drive degradation is a known issue on higher-mileage units. Rebuilt CRGC engines include a decarbonised head, new water pump, upgraded oil pump drive, and new timing set.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRGC 1.8 TSI engine replacement

entries.title: CHHA 2.0 TSI 190 Petrol
entries.description: The CHHA 2.0 TSI (EA888 Gen3, 190hp) powers the Octavia (5E), Superb (3V), Kodiaq (NS), and Karoq (NU). Carbon buildup on intake valves is the #1 long-term issue. Water pump leaks and thermostat housing cracks are also common. Rebuilt CHHA units include walnut-blasted valves, new water pump/thermostat housing, revised PCV, and new timing chain set.

entries.title: CRTD 2.0 TFSI Petrol
entries.description: The CRTD is the performance variant (220–230hp) found in the Octavia vRS (5E, 2013–2020) and Superb (3V, 2015–2020). Same carbon buildup issues as CHHA, plus increased risk of timing chain stretch on tuned examples. Rebuilt CRTD units include full decarbonisation, upgraded timing chain kit, new water pump, and performance-oriented head gasket (where applicable).
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRTD 2.0 TFSI engine replacement

entries.title: DNUB 2.0 TSI 190 Petrol
entries.description: The DNUB is a later EA888 Gen3B Miller-cycle engine (190hp) with improved thermal efficiency. Fitted to the Octavia (5E FL, 2017–2020), Kodiaq (NS), and Karoq (NU). Carbon buildup still occurs but takes longer to cause symptoms. Water pump leaks remain common. Rebuilt DNUB units include decarbonised valves, new water pump assembly, revised PCV, and new timing chain kit.

entries.title: BNM 1.4 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The BNM three-cylinder 1.4 TDI (75–80hp) powered the Fabia (5J, 2005–2014), Roomster (2006–2015), and Rapid (NH, 2012–2014). Injector loom failure inside the cylinder head causes misfires and limp mode. EGR valve clogging and turbo actuator failure are also common. Rebuilt BNM units include a new injector loom, cleaned EGR valve, and reconditioned turbo actuator.
entries.cta: Get quotes for BNM 1.4 TDI engine

entries.title: BLS 1.9 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The BLS 1.9 TDI PD (Pumpe Düse) was fitted to the Octavia (1Z, 2004–2010), Fabia (5J, 2007–2010), and Roomster. Camshaft lobe wear (where the cam actuates the unit injectors) and in-head injector wiring loom failure are the two dominant replacement drivers. Rebuilt BLS units include a new camshaft, new injector loom, and new timing belt kit.

entries.title: AZQ 1.2 HTP Petrol
entries.description: The AZQ 1.2 HTP (6-valve) was fitted to the Fabia (5J, 2003–2010) and Roomster (2006–2010). A simple 3-cylinder, chain-driven unit. Valve seat recession (running on LPG or poor maintenance) and timing chain stretch are the main issues. Rebuilt AZQ units include a new timing chain kit, re-cut valve seats, and new valve stem seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for AZQ 1.2 HTP engine replacement

engineYears.h2: Skoda Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers Skoda's complete UK engine timeline from 1998 to the present across every major model line - from the pre-VW Group 1.9 SDI/TDI era through the landmark EA111/EA113 petrol families, the high-demand 1.9 TDI and 2.0 TDI diesels, the EA888 TSI turbo petrol generation, the EA211/EA288 modular era, and the current iV PHEV and Enyaq electric range - so owners can identify exactly what was fitted to their vehicle by year and compare rebuilt Skoda engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: VW Group Integration Bedding In: 1.9 TDI & 1.6/2.0 MPI Era
years.description: The post-VW Group integration Skoda range was built around Volkswagen's proven EA827/EA111 petrol and the 1.9 SDI/TDI diesel families - giving Skoda buyers access to VW Group reliability for the first time across the Octavia, Fabia, and Felicia.
keyChanges: First-generation Octavia (1996 launch, UK volume from 1998) - 1.6 MPI, 1.8 20v, 2.0 MPI, 1.9 SDI, 1.9 TDI
Fabia first generation launched (1999) - 1.4 MPI, 1.0 MPI, 1.9 SDI, 1.9 TDI (100 hp)
1.9 TDI PD (Pumpe Düse unit injector) introduced across the VW Group range - significant power increase over pre-PD TDI
Felicia continues until 2001 with 1.3 MPI and 1.9 D/SDI - final non-VW engine Skoda
Euro 2/3 compliance in force
mainEngines: AGR / AHF / ASV - 1.9 TDI (90 hp) (Octavia, Fabia diesel)
ALH - 1.9 TDI (110 hp) (Octavia vRS TDI)
AEE / AKK - 1.6 MPI (Octavia, Fabia)
AHL / AEH - 1.6 petrol (Octavia)
AQY / APK - 2.0 MPI petrol (Octavia)
AGP / AYQ - 1.9 SDI (Fabia, Octavia)
ARX / AUM - 1.8 20v turbo (Octavia 1.8T)
knownFor: 1.9 TDI (pre-PD) is one of the most reliable diesel engines ever produced - robust pump-and-injector design, exceptional longevity; UK specialists regularly see examples exceeding 250,000 miles with correct timing belt servicing

Timing belt failure on 1.9 TDI is catastrophic and completely preventable - belt service every 60,000 miles (not the stated 90,000) is the UK specialist consensus for peace of mind; this single maintenance item generates the highest volume of Skoda engine replacement enquiries for this era
1.8 20v turbo Octavia is a capable, enjoyable engine - early units suffer oil sludge from extended service intervals but long-lived when properly maintained
1.9 SDI naturally aspirated diesel is notably underpowered but virtually indestructible - preferred choice for ultra-high-mileage fleet operators due to its simplicity
years.cta: Find rebuilt 1998–2001 Skoda 1.9 TDI and 1.8T engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Superb First Generation, 1.9 TDI PD Peak & 1.4 16v Introduction
years.description: The first-generation Superb extended Skoda upmarket with VW Group B-segment platform engines, the Octavia RS debuted the 1.8T in performance trim, and the 1.9 TDI PD (Pumpe Düse) unit-injector diesel became the most widely fitted Skoda diesel in UK history.
keyChanges: Superb first generation (2002) - first Skoda executive saloon; 1.8T, 2.5 TDI V6 (BDG), and 2.8 V6 petrol
1.9 TDI PD (BKC, BXE, BLS) replaces pre-PD units across Octavia and Fabia - 100/105 hp
Octavia RS launched with 1.8T 180 hp - benchmark Skoda performance model
Fabia continues with 1.4 16v (BBY, BKY) petrol and 1.9 TDI (AMF 75 hp) diesel
Room for Yeti previewed on Octavia platform
Euro 3 dominant; Euro 4 preparation begins
mainEngines: BKC / BXE / BLS - 1.9 TDI PD 100–105 hp (Octavia, Fabia, Superb)
AMF - 1.9 TDI PD 100 hp (Fabia 100)
AWT / ARX - 1.8 20v turbo 150/180 hp (Octavia RS, Superb 1.8T)
BDG - 2.5 TDI V6 (Superb 2.5 TDI - VW Group unit)
AZX - 2.8 V6 petrol (Superb 2.8)
BBY / BKY - 1.4 16v petrol (Fabia)
BFQ / BFJ - 1.6 MPI petrol (Octavia, Fabia)
knownFor: 1.9 TDI PD units (BKC, BXE, BLS) are the UK's most commercially significant Skoda diesel replacement engines - extremely widespread, excellent long-term durability, but timing belt replacement at 60,000-mile intervals remains absolutely critical; this engine generates peak UK Skoda replacement search volume across this period
1.9 TDI PD is susceptible to EGR valve carbon fouling in urban use - EGR blanking or regular chemical cleaning is a standard UK independent specialist recommendation for high-mileage examples
2.5 TDI V6 Superb is a smooth and capable unit but cam belt failures and oil leaks from front seals are the most cited concerns - considered higher-maintenance than the 1.9 TDI
1.8T 180 hp Octavia RS is a well-regarded performance engine - coil pack failure and oil consumption from high-mileage PCV system wear are the most common UK specialist diagnoses

years.preview: Octavia Second Generation, 2.0 TDI & EA113 2.0 TSI Introduction Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The second-generation Octavia (2004 launch, UK volume from 2005) brought VW Group's new EA113 2.0 TSI turbocharged petrol and the EA188 2.0 TDI CR common-rail diesel - an engineering step change that defined the Skoda range for the following decade.
keyChanges: Octavia second generation (2004 global, 2005 UK dominant) - 1.6 MPI, 1.8 TSI, 2.0 TDI CR, 1.9 TDI PD
2.0 TDI CR (AZV, BKD) introduced - common-rail replaces PD unit injector in Octavia and Superb; 140 hp
2.0 TSI EA113 (AXX, BWA, BPY) introduced in Octavia vRS (200 hp) - turbocharged direct-injection four-cylinder
Roomster launched (2006) with 1.4 TDI (BMS, BNV) and 1.6 MPI (BSF)
Superb second generation previewed
Euro 4 enforced from 2006
mainEngines: AZV / BKD - 2.0 TDI CR 140 hp (Octavia, Superb)
BXE / BKC - 1.9 TDI PD 105 hp (Octavia, Fabia continued)
AXX / BWA / BPY - 2.0 TSI EA113 200 hp (Octavia vRS)
BSE / BSF - 1.6 MPI (Octavia, Roomster)
BMD - 1.4 TSI (Octavia, Fabia preview)
BMS / BNV - 1.4 TDI PD (Roomster, Fabia diesel)
knownFor: 2.0 TDI CR (BKD, AZV) is a significant reliability improvement over PD units for some failure modes - but timing belt still required at 60,000–75,000 miles; EGR valve and DPF fouling in urban use are the primary UK specialist complaints

2.0 TSI EA113 (AXX, BWA) in Octavia vRS suffers from the notorious early timing chain tensioner fault - plastic collar on the original tensioner can fail, allowing chain to jump and destroy the engine; this is one of the most significant VW Group petrol engine reliability incidents of its era

1.9 TDI PD continues to generate strong UK replacement demand as the large pre-2005 fleet ages - 2005–2007 period is the peak year for 1.9 TDI engine failure and replacement across the entire Skoda range
1.4 TDI PD in Roomster and Fabia is a compact, fuel-efficient unit but timing belt failure and injector wear at higher mileages are well-documented

years.preview: Superb Second Gen, Yeti, 1.4 TSI Twincharged & EA888 Arrives Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The second-generation Superb and the Yeti expanded the Skoda range significantly, the 1.4 TSI twincharged engine introduced Skoda's most technically innovative petrol unit, and the EA888 1.8/2.0 TSI family began replacing EA113 as VW Group's volume turbocharged petrol.
keyChanges: Superb second generation (2008) - 1.8 TSI (EA888), 2.0 TDI CR, 3.6 V6 FSI - Skoda's largest and most sophisticated model
Yeti launched (2009) - first Skoda SUV; 1.2 TSI, 1.8 TSI, 2.0 TDI CR
1.4 TSI twincharged (CAXA, BMY) - supercharger + turbocharger; 125/140 hp; Fabia vRS and Octavia
EA888 1.8 TSI (BZB, CDAA, CABA) introduced - replaces EA113 1.8T across volume models
EA189 2.0 TDI CR updated (CBAB, CFFB) - replaces BKD/AZV in Octavia, Superb, Yeti
Euro 5 preparation begins
mainEngines: CDAA / CABA / BZB - 1.8 TSI EA888 Gen 1 (Octavia, Superb)
CAXA / BMY - 1.4 TSI twincharged 125/140 hp (Fabia vRS, Octavia 1.4)
CBAB / CFFB - 2.0 TDI CR EA189 140/170 hp (Octavia, Superb, Yeti)
CFGB / CLCA - 2.0 TDI 140/170 hp (Superb, Yeti)
AXX continued / CDLF - 2.0 TSI EA113/EA888 200 hp (Octavia vRS)
CHFA / CGPA - 1.2 TSI (Yeti, Fabia - EA111)
knownFor: EA888 Gen 1 1.8 TSI timing chain is significantly more reliable than EA113 - however early Gen 1 units (2008–2011) still show chain stretch and tensioner wear from 80,000–100,000 miles; cold-start chain rattle is the first warning sign

1.4 TSI twincharged is a technical marvel - supercharger for instant low-end response, turbo takes over higher in the rev range; however supercharger drive belt and clutch wear from 60,000 miles makes this a higher-maintenance petrol option; timing belt failure remains catastrophic

EA189 2.0 TDI CR introduces Dieselgate-era emissions software - while not directly an engine failure, 2008–2015 EA189 diesels are the focus of the NOx defeat device scandal (VW Group, 2015); no mechanical impact on engine reliability but EGR and DPF modifications from the fix can affect performance
Yeti 2.0 TDI is a very capable and practical car - 4x4 versions with CBAB/CFFB units considered among the most trouble-free UK diesel crossovers of this era

years.preview: Octavia Third Generation Preview, Dieselgate EA189 Era & Euro 5 Peak Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The EA111 1.2 TSI became Skoda's most widely fitted entry petrol engine, the EA188/EA189 2.0 TDI range reached peak UK market penetration across Octavia, Superb, and Yeti, and the Octavia third generation was previewed with full EA211/EA288 engine preparation.
keyChanges: EA111 1.2 TSI (CBZB, CBZA, CJZA) dominant in Fabia, Yeti, and Roomster - 86/105 hp turbocharged three/four-cylinder
Rapid launched (2012) - new model; 1.2 TSI and 1.6 TDI CR
Octavia third generation revealed (2012) - full launch 2013; EA211 and EA288 preview
Dieselgate EA189 2.0 TDI continues - CBAB, CFFB, CJAA variants; 110/140/170 hp
1.4 TSI CTHD (EA111) and CXSA (EA211) transition begins in Octavia
Euro 5 in force; Euro 6 preparation from 2013
mainEngines: CBZB / CBZA / CJZA - 1.2 TSI EA111 86/105 hp (Fabia, Yeti, Roomster, Rapid)
CFFB / CBAB / CJAA - 2.0 TDI CR EA189 110/140 hp (Octavia, Superb, Yeti)
CLCA / CFGB - 2.0 TDI 170 hp (Superb, Yeti 4x4)
CTHE / CTHG - 1.4 TSI 122 hp (Octavia pre-EA211)
CDAA / CDAB - 1.8 TSI EA888 Gen 2 (Superb, Octavia)
CUPA - 2.0 TSI 220 hp (Octavia vRS)
popularModels: Skoda Octavia (second generation final / third generation preview)
knownFor: EA189 2.0 TDI in Octavia, Superb, and Yeti represents the most commercially significant UK Skoda diesel period - enormous volume on UK roads; EGR valve and DPF maintenance the primary ongoing cost; Dieselgate emissions fix (applied from 2016) can sometimes lead to increased EGR deposits and DPF frequency issues

1.2 TSI EA111 is frugal and practical but prone to timing chain wear on early CBZB units - chain stretch and cam cover oil leaks from 60,000 miles; cylinder head bolt failure on overheated examples is a more serious and documented concern

EA888 Gen 2 1.8 TSI (CDAA/CDAB) improves on Gen 1 reliability - revised oil pump and chain tensioner; but oil consumption from piston ring wear remains a known characteristic, especially in high-performance applications
Rapid 1.2 TSI generates growing UK replacement demand as fleet passes 80,000–100,000 miles - timing chain, cam cover gasket, and coil pack failure are the three most common enquiries

years.preview: EA211 & EA288 Modular Engine Launch, Octavia Third Gen & Dieselgate Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The introduction of the EA211 petrol and EA288 diesel modular engine families across the Octavia third generation and Karoq/Kodiaq platform marked Skoda's most comprehensive engine architecture renewal in 20 years - coinciding with the Dieselgate scandal that reshaped the diesel market.
keyChanges: EA211 1.0 TSI three-cylinder (CHZB, DKRA) and 1.4 TSI (CZCA, CXSA) launched - modular family replacing EA111 across all Skoda small/medium models
EA288 2.0 TDI (CRBC, CRKB, DCYA) replaces EA189 - revised diesel with timing belt replacing chain; Euro 6 compliant
Dieselgate defeat device scandal (September 2015) - EA189 1.6/2.0 TDI across all Skoda models affected; VW Group software fix roll-out begins 2016
Octavia third generation fully dominant with EA211/EA288 range - 1.0 TSI, 1.4 TSI, 1.8 TSI, 2.0 TDI
Kodiaq revealed (2016) - largest Skoda ever; 1.4 TSI, 2.0 TSI, 2.0 TDI EA288
AdBlue/SCR introduced on EA288 2.0 TDI from Euro 6 - September 2015 new registrations
mainEngines: CHZB / DKRA - 1.0 TSI EA211 three-cylinder 95/115 hp (Fabia, Octavia, Rapid, Scala)
CZCA / CXSA / CZDA - 1.4 TSI EA211 125/150 hp (Octavia, Superb)
CJSA / CJSB - 1.8 TSI EA888 Gen 3 180 hp (Octavia vRS)
CRBC / CRKB - 2.0 TDI EA288 115/150 hp (Octavia, Superb, Kodiaq)
DCYA / DTSB - 2.0 TDI EA288 190 hp (Superb, Kodiaq 4x4)
CZPB - 2.0 TSI EA888 Gen 3 220/245 hp (Octavia vRS, Kodiaq vRS preview)
knownFor: EA288 2.0 TDI is a competent Euro 6 diesel - DPF blockage in short-journey urban use, EGR valve fouling, AdBlue pump failure, and turbocharger wear are the four most commonly cited UK specialist concerns; timing belt (not chain) service interval critical

EA211 1.0 TSI three-cylinder is Skoda's most efficient and refined small engine - frugal and smooth but oil consumption from piston ring wear and timing chain wear from 70,000 miles on early units are the growing UK specialist concerns
EA888 Gen 3 1.8/2.0 TSI improves on Gen 1/2 reliability - revised oil control and chain tensioner; oil consumption and PCV valve failure remain known characteristics on high-mileage examples

Dieselgate fallout depresses used EA189 values significantly - creates commercial opportunity for UK independent diesel specialists handling Skoda owners who retain cars and seek independent engine maintenance rather than dealer network engagement

years.preview: Karoq, Kodiaq, Euro 6d & 1.5 TSI Evo Introduction Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: The Karoq replaced the Yeti, the Kodiaq reached full UK sales volume, and the new 1.5 TSI Evo with cylinder deactivation replaced the 1.4 TSI - a significant mid-cycle engine update that improved efficiency while introducing new ownership considerations.
keyChanges: Karoq launched (2017) - replaces Yeti; 1.0 TSI, 1.5 TSI Evo, 2.0 TDI EA288
1.5 TSI Evo (DADA, DPCA) replaces 1.4 TSI - cylinder deactivation (ACT) technology; improved fuel economy
Kodiaq fully established in UK market with 2.0 TDI and 2.0 TSI options
Scala and Kamiq launched (2019) - 1.0 TSI, 1.5 TSI, 1.6 TDI
EA288 TDI updated to Euro 6d-TEMP - revised EGR, DPF, and AdBlue systems
Octavia continues on EA211/EA288 - receives mild cosmetic updates
mainEngines: DADA / DPCA - 1.5 TSI Evo cylinder deactivation 130/150 hp (Karoq, Octavia, Kodiaq)
DKRA / CHZB - 1.0 TSI EA211 115 hp (Karoq, Scala, Kamiq, Fabia)
CJSA updated - 1.8 TSI EA888 Gen 3 180 hp (Octavia RS continued)
CRBC / CRKB / DCYA - 2.0 TDI EA288 (Karoq, Kodiaq, Superb - Euro 6d-TEMP)
CZPB / DGEA - 2.0 TSI EA888 Gen 3 (Kodiaq vRS, Octavia vRS)
knownFor: 1.5 TSI Evo cylinder deactivation is efficient but introduces a known issue - the cylinder deactivation actuator on some early DADA units can stick, causing a lumpy idle at low speed; revised software and hardware updates address most cases from 2019 onwards

2.0 TDI EA288 AdBlue system failures are accelerating as first-generation Euro 6d vehicles age into the 50,000–80,000-mile window - AdBlue injector, pump, and tank heater failures are the most commercially significant UK Skoda diesel specialist growth area

EA888 Gen 3 2.0 TSI in Kodiaq vRS and Octavia vRS is the best iteration of this engine family - oil consumption remains a known characteristic but significantly reduced versus Gen 1/2; timing chain tensioner concerns largely resolved with factory-spec oil changes

Karoq 1.0 TSI is the UK's most practical Skoda petrol in this era - reliable, frugal, easy to run; timing chain wear from 70,000 miles and oil consumption on harder-used examples are the primary growing concerns

years.preview: Octavia Fourth Generation, Enyaq iV & Octavia iV PHEV Technology Introduction
years.description: The fourth-generation Octavia brought mild hybrid (MHEV) 48V technology to Skoda petrol engines for the first time, the Octavia iV PHEV extended EV range to 55 miles WLTP, and the Enyaq iV marked Skoda's first dedicated BEV on the MEB platform.
keyChanges: Octavia fourth generation (2020) - 1.0 TSI, 1.5 TSI eTSI (48V MHEV), 2.0 TSI, 2.0 TDI; first Octavia with MHEV
1.5 TSI eTSI (DPBA) - 48V belt-starter-generator added to 1.5 TSI; mild hybrid system new to Skoda
Octavia iV PHEV (2021) - 1.4 TSI PHEV + 85 kW electric motor + 13 kWh battery; up to 55 miles EV
Enyaq iV (2021) - first dedicated Skoda BEV; MEB platform; 58/82 kWh battery; RWD/AWD
Fabia fourth generation (2021) - EA211 Evo 1.0 TSI and 1.5 TSI; no diesel option
Euro 6d final compliance from January 2021 - OPF (Otto Particle Filter) on all new petrol models
mainEngines: DPBA / DADA Evo - 1.5 TSI eTSI 48V MHEV 150 hp (Octavia, Karoq, Kodiaq)
DKLA / DLAA - 1.0 TSI Evo 110 hp OPF (Fabia, Octavia, Kamiq, Scala)
DNFA / DFYA - 1.4 TSI PHEV (Octavia iV - combustion side)
DTSA / DTSB - 2.0 TDI EA288 Evo 115/150 hp (Octavia, Kodiaq, Superb)
CZPB / DGEA - 2.0 TSI EA888 Gen 3 245 hp (Octavia vRS, Kodiaq vRS)
Enyaq Electric Motor - 148/204/265 hp BEV (Enyaq iV, Enyaq iV 80)
knownFor: 1.5 TSI eTSI 48V MHEV delivers measurable fuel economy improvement in mixed driving - 48V belt-starter-generator smooth and effective; however the MHEV system's belt-starter-generator (BSG) unit is an emerging UK specialist concern as early 2020–2021 cars approach service age

Octavia iV PHEV 1.4 TSI plug-in hybrid is a strong proposition for company car and high-mileage users - 55 miles EV range, low BIK taxation; combustion engine itself is reliable but the PHEV inverter and high-voltage battery conditioning require specialist-grade knowledge from independent workshops

Enyaq iV MEB platform BEV is Skoda's most acclaimed product launch in a generation - UK owners consistently report reliability, but 12V auxiliary battery failure and software update interruptions are the most common Enyaq independent specialist topics in 2023–2025

OPF Otto Particle Filter on all 2021+ petrol models adds a new maintenance category - oil dilution in DPF/OPF regeneration cycles on short-journey cars is a growing UK concern for 1.0 TSI and 1.5 TSI owners

years.preview: Kodiaq Second Generation, Superb Fifth Generation & Electrified Expansion
years.description: Skoda's current UK range runs on EA211 Evo petrol, EA288 Evo diesel, and 48V MHEV systems across a new Kodiaq and Superb generation - while the Enyaq and Enyaq Coupé cement Skoda's BEV credentials and combustion engine replacement demand grows for the large 2014–2020 fleet.

faq.h2: Skoda Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: ** Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Skoda owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Skoda engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement Skoda engine typically costs between £1,200 and £2,800 for common units like the 1.9 TDI (BXE/BLS) or 1.2 TSI (CBZA), rising to £3,000–£5,500 for the 2.0 TDI (CKFC/CRLB) or 2.0 TSI (CHHA) used in Octavia vRS and Kodiaq. Reconditioned engines usually run £1,800–£3,500 fitted, while supply‑only can knock £400–£800 off the total. The final price varies significantly by engine code and condition — compare multiple quotes to ensure you're not overpaying.
items.cta: Compare Skoda engine replacement prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Skoda engine replacement?
items.answer: Four main factors drive the price: engine size and family (1.2 TSI EA111 costs far less than 2.0 TSI EA888), condition (used vs rebuilt vs remanufactured), vehicle age and complexity (Kodiaq 4x4 takes more labour than Fabia FWD), and whether you choose supply‑only or full supply‑and‑fit. London and South East garages typically charge 20–30% more than Northern or Midlands specialists for the same job. Our comparison tool brings transparency to these variables — you'll see exactly what each supplier includes.
items.cta: Get quotes from UK Skoda engine specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Skoda?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if the chassis, gearbox, and bodywork are solid. A professionally rebuilt Skoda engine with upgraded components (e.g., revised timing chain tensioner on EA111 1.2/1.4 TSI) will often outlast the original and costs far less than replacing the entire vehicle. The alternative is buying a used car of unknown history, which carries its own risks. If your Skoda holds reasonable residual value (e.g., Octavia vRS, Superb Laurin & Klement), engine replacement is almost always the smarter financial decision.
items.cta: Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Skoda engine makes financial sense

items.question: What's included in a Skoda engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit package typically includes sourcing the correct engine code for your Skoda, removal of the failed unit, installation of the replacement engine, new oil, filters, coolant, a workmanship warranty (usually 12 months), disposal of the old engine, and a road test to confirm proper operation. Fitting costs generally add £400–£800 to the supply price, depending on model complexity. Always confirm what's included — some quotes exclude ancillaries like the turbo or EGR cooler.
items.cta: Compare Skoda engine supply and fit deals

items.question: How long does a Skoda engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common Skoda models like the Fabia 1.2 TSI or Octavia 2.0 TDI, a like‑for‑like swap typically takes 3–5 working days from order to road‑ready. More complex models (Kodiaq vRS, Superb 4x4) may extend to 5–7 days due to additional drivetrain components. The biggest variable is engine availability — our network of UK specialists stocks frequently‑replaced codes (e.g., CAYC 1.6 TDI, CBZA 1.2 TSI, CKFC 2.0 TDI), but rarer units may add 2–3 days for delivery. Ask each supplier for their current lead time before committing.
items.cta: Find reconditioned Skoda engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: Do reconditioned Skoda engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes — every reconditioned Skoda engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Many rebuilders also offer extended 24‑month coverage on remanufactured units that include new pistons, rings, and timing components. The warranty typically covers major internal parts: crankshaft, con rods, pistons, cylinder head, timing chain/belt, oil pump, and gaskets. Always confirm exact terms, labour coverage limits, and any exclusions (e.g., injectors, turbo, ancillary components) directly with your chosen supplier before purchase.
items.cta: Compare Skoda engine replacement prices now

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Skoda engine?
items.answer: Used engines are cheaper upfront (often £600–£1,200 less) and make sense as a temporary fix or if you plan to sell the car within a year. However, you inherit the original failure risks — including known Skoda issues like EA111 timing chain stretch or EA288 DPF clogging. Reconditioned engines cost more (£1,800–£3,500 fitted) but include new wear components (rings, bearings, seals, timing kit) and a 12‑month warranty, typically delivering another 100k+ miles. On failure‑prone Skoda engines (1.2 TSI, 1.4 TSI, 1.6 TDI), a reconditioned unit with upgraded parts is strongly recommended over a used donor.

items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK‑wide network of independent specialists who regularly rebuild and replace Skoda engines — from 1.2 TSI Fabia units to 2.0 TDI Superb and 2.0 TSI vRS engines. While the Midlands and South East have particular concentrations of VAG specialists, our platform covers the entire UK, and many suppliers offer nationwide delivery with insured palletised shipping. Simply enter your registration above to identify your exact engine code and be matched with nearby (or national) specialists who stock that unit and can arrange fitting at your location.

items.question: What are the most common Skoda engine failures?
items.answer: The most common catastrophic failures driving Skoda engine replacement include: EA111 1.2/1.4 TSI timing chain tensioner failure (Fabia, Rapid, Yeti, 2010–2014), EA189 1.6 TDI DPF/EGR clogging leading to turbo backpressure failure (Octavia, Yeti, 2010–2015), EA288 2.0 TDI oil pump belt snapping on evo versions (Superb, Kodiaq, 2019–2022), EA888 Gen1/2 2.0 TSI timing chain stretch and intake carbon buildup (Octavia vRS, 2008–2013), and 1.4 TSI piston ring carbon clogging causing high oil consumption (Fabia vRS, Yeti, 2010–2016). Early detection rarely prevents these from becoming terminal — replacement is the most common outcome.
items.cta: Compare Skoda engine replacement prices for these common failure codes

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Skoda engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes — prices for the same Skoda engine code can vary by £1,000+ between suppliers. Consider used (£800–£1,800) for tight budgets or short‑term ownership, but upgrade to a reconditioned unit (£1,800–£3,500) with a 12‑month warranty if you plan to keep the car. Ask whether the quote is supply‑only or includes fitting (fitters charge £400–£800 extra), whether delivery and VAT are included, and whether the old engine disposal is covered. Avoid the cheapest offer if it excludes warranty or uses unknown‑history donor engines. Transparency saves money.
